title: Delve - A Map Making Drawing Game
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=coqZMUFd2C0

Daniel (@dungeondive) opens with nostalgia — those childhood pencil-and-paper games of drawing bases and dungeons — to frame Delve, a solo map-making drawing game by Anna Blackwell (one of a trio with Rise and Umbra). You play a dwarven hold digging ever deeper, drawing a unique side-view stronghold room by room as you go. He cites lineage from How to Host a Dungeon and Microscope: this is a slow, relaxing, podcast-friendly creative activity more than a tense game.

Rules are light and driven by a standard card deck: each turn you pick a square to explore and flip a card. Hearts and diamonds yield resources and trade goods (scaled by depth); spades are hostile "remnants" (demon portals, burrowing beasts); clubs are natural formations. Discoveries get drawn onto your map and spent on rooms, traps and recruits, with lightweight strength-vs-strength combat as creatures try to claw up to the surface. He shows off his own in-progress hold — barracks, an overseer's office, a healing library built in truce with insect-folk — and admits he's there for the drawing and the story, not the combat. His wish: a bigger sheet, and a sample playthrough in the book. A lovely, low-key world-builder.

----

Delve is less a game to win than a quiet, relaxing act of drawing a world into being. Do you have a low-stakes "creative activity" game you reach for to unwind, rather than to be challenged?
